coredata.dc:title: High recovery speed optical humidity sensing enabled with GQD for non-contact finger distance detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yufei
Wang, Fuxiang
Gao, Feng
Kang, Jianing
Wu, Jun
Ren, Ziqiao
Zhou, Jun
Dong, Jie
Zhou, Wenjun
Shen, Changyu
coredata.dc:description: Finger distance perception plays a crucial role in enabling humanoid intelligent robotic systems to achieve smooth interaction with humans. Here, a rapidly response optical humidity sensor enabled with graphene quantum dots (GQD) for non-contact finger distance detection was proposed. The GQD was prepared through the functionalization of graphene oxide (GO) with hydrophilic groups. This modification enhances the responsiveness of GO to atmospheric humidity and promotes the deposition of conformal films of functional materials on the surfaces of optical fiber. Utilizing a light-driven coating method, the GQD was deposited onto a tilted fiber Bragg grating (TFBG) to fabricate humidity sensor. When water molecules were absorbed by GQD, the dielectric constant of GQD film was changed, causing an alteration in the intensities of the TFBG’s cladding modes. The proposed sensor shows a sensitivity of 0.032 dB/%RH with the equilibrium response and recovery time of 3.66 s and 0.92 s, respectively. The equilibrium recovery time is the fastest as far as we know. Furthermore, the sensor is capable of precisely identifying the action of a finger, such as approaching, remaining, and departing with a detection range of 0–10 mm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-contact sensing
Fiber humidity sensor
Tilted fiber Bragg grating
Graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthesis process of GQD; (b) EDS of GQD; (c) Configuration of the humidity sensor; (d) SEM of TFBG surface; (e) The experimental setup for RH detection.

(a)Transmission spectra of GQD coated TFBG with the RH fixed at 20 % and 90 %; (b) Different RH levels against intensities of the cladding mode at 1516.45 nm; Linear fitting of (c) the core mode, the cladding mode intensity at 1516.45 nm with (d) RH decreasing and (e) RH increasing; (f) Intensities difference of the resonant wavelength of 1516.45 nm between RH increasing and decreasing.

(a) Response time tests; (b) Comparison of the response time and recovery time with different materials coated with optical fiber sensor; (c) Stability test results at two fixed RH levels (40 % and 80 %); (d) Intensities of the resonant wavelength of 1516.45 nm in one week stability tests.
(a) Schematic diagram of non-contact finger distance detection; (b) Responses of GQD coated TFBG to non-contact finger distance detection.

head.ce:title: High recovery speed optical humidity sensing enabled with GQD for non-contact finger distance detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Finger distance perception plays a crucial role in enabling humanoid intelligent robotic systems to achieve smooth interaction with humans. Here, a rapidly response optical humidity sensor enabled with graphene quantum dots (GQD) for non-contact finger distance detection was proposed. The GQD was prepared through the functionalization of graphene oxide (GO) with hydrophilic groups. This modification enhances the responsiveness of GO to atmospheric humidity and promotes the deposition of conformal films of functional materials on the surfaces of optical fiber. Utilizing a light-driven coating method, the GQD was deposited onto a tilted fiber Bragg grating (TFBG) to fabricate humidity sensor. When water molecules were absorbed by GQD, the dielectric constant of GQD film was changed, causing an alteration in the intensities of the TFBG’s cladding modes. The proposed sensor shows a sensitivity of 0.032 dB/%RH with the equilibrium response and recovery time of 3.66 s and 0.92 s, respectively. The equilibrium recovery time is the fastest as far as we know. Furthermore, the sensor is capable of precisely identifying the action of a finger, such as approaching, remaining, and departing with a detection range of 0–10 mm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-contact sensors are widely used in various fields such as automation, healthcare, and security. For the rapidly advancing field of humanoid robotics, the development of non-contact finger distance perception sensors, which can precisely refine the actions of robots to facilitate smooth interaction with humans, is a research focus with promising application prospects [1]. Common non-contact sensing sensors include infrared sensors [2], ultrasonic sensors [3], capacitive touch sensors [4], and humidity sensors [5]. These sensors can detect the presence, distance, temperature, position, and touch of objects, providing timely responses and feedback. Accounting for more than 60 % of human body weight, water contributes to the formation of a natural humidity field surrounding the body through evaporation. Therefore, the measurement of this humidity field permits the indirect acquisition of data regarding the movement and positioning of the fingers. Consequently, the measurement of humidity is recognized as a crucial approach for the non-contact detection of finger position at a micro scale.

Optical fiber sensors show many advantages in humidity detections [6–10]. Firstly, based on the principle of optical waveguides, they exhibit extremely high sensitivity and accuracy, enabling precise detection of humidity levels. Secondly, owing to the electromagnetic interference resistance of optical fibers, these sensors can operate normally in strong electromagnetic field environments, ensuring the accuracy and stability of measurements. Additionally, the small size and lightweight nature of optical fibers make them easy to carry and install. The performance of optical fiber humidity sensors is influenced by the fiber structure and humidity-sensitive materials. Fiber optic humidity sensors can generally be categorized into three types: evanescent field-based sensor, interferometric fiber optic sensor, fiber grating sensor. Evanescent field-based humidity sensors track changes in moisture concentration by measuring the interaction between the evanescent field and the humidity-sensitive material coated on the surface of fiber. Currently, advancements in evanescent field-based humidity sensors have involved the integration of specialized fibers, including side-polished fiber [11], micro-nano fiber [12], and U-bending fiber [13,14]. Moreover, interferometric fiber optic humidity sensors not only detect alterations in the refractive index of humidity sensitive functional materials via the evanescent field but also utilize interference structures to enhance the signal response. Typical configurations of these interference structures include the Fabry-Perot interferometer [15,16], Michelson interferometer [17], Mach-Zehnder interferometer [18–21], and Sagnac interferometer [22].

Furthermore, owing to their controllable architecture, ease of fabrication, and robust stability, fiber grating structures, including fiber Bragg grating (FBG) [23] and long period grating (LPG) [24] are extensively employed in the fabrication of fiber optic sensors. Leveraging the inherent merits of grating structures, TFBG angle the grating at a tilt to the fiber cross-section, thereby exciting more cladding modes and significantly enhancing sensitivity to refractive index measurements [25]. Consequently, the TFBG represents one of the optimal optical structures for the development of high-performance fiber optic humidity sensors [26–30].

The performance of humidity-sensitive materials directly influences the sensitivity, response time, repeatability, and stability of sensors. Common humidity-sensitive materials include inorganic oxides, organic polymers, and carbon-based materials. Inorganic oxides such as zirconium dioxide [31] and tin dioxide [32] change their electrical properties through adsorption-desorption of water molecules to achieve humidity detection. However, their performance variation range is small, which may result in lower sensitivity. Organic polymers such as PVA [33], PMMA [34], PI [35], utilize hydrophilic groups in their molecular structure to adsorb water molecules, causing changes in dielectric constant, and are suitable for capacitive and optical fiber humidity sensors. However, prolonged exposure to high humidity environments may lead to performance degradation. Carbon-based materials such as graphene and fullerene, when modified with hydrophilic groups to become humidity-sensitive materials, exhibit a wide humidity range, fast response, and good stability [36–39]. In 2016, Wang et al. deposited Graphene oxide (GO) at TFBG, and the intensity of cladding modes changed with the process of GO adsorption-desorption of water molecules [40]. A maximum sensitivity of 0.129 dB/%RH was obtained in the relative humidity (RH) range of 10–80 %. GO extensively employed in fabricating optical fiber humidity sensors, exhibits an excellent response to humidity. Nevertheless, challenges in achieving film formation on the fiber surface conformally are presented by its flake structure and insolubility in water. The synthesis of graphene quantum dots via lysine modification of graphene represents a significant approach to addressing the challenges associated with film formation [41]. Notably, Lysine contains two amino groups and one carboxyl group. It will enhance the humidity-sensitive of the GO. Additionally, utilizing the hydrophilic-hydrophobic self-assembly principle, GQD colloidal dispersion solution can be formed. In this colloidal state, the humidity-sensitive functional material is more conducive to conformal film formation on the fiber surface.

Here, we proposed a rapidly response optical humidity sensor enabled with GQD for non-contact finger distance detection. The colloidal suspension of GQD was prepared by grafting lysine onto the GO. This modification not only improved the water solubility, thereby enhancing the adhesion between the carbon-based material and the optical fiber, but also enhanced the hydrophilicity of graphene oxide, which in turn increased the refractive index change of GQD. The humidity was fabricated by coating the GQDs on a TFBG uniformly by light-driven deposition. The intensity of the TFBG cladding modes was altered by the refractive index change of GQD when it was exposed to different humidity environments. The sensor shows a sensitivity of 0.032 dB/%RH over a range of 40–90 % RH. In addition, the sensor has a fast response and recovery time of 3.66 s and 0.92 s, respectively. Finally, the sensor was used for non-contact sensing applications, and it can identify actions of a finger, including approaching, staying, and departing, within a maximum response range of 10 mm.

The preparation method of GQD [42] is illustrated in Fig. 1(a). The GO (95 %) was purchased from Shenzhen Hongdachang Electronic Materials Co., Ltd. Ethanol (99.5 %), L-lysine (98 %), and Sodium hydroxide (AR, 96 %) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Tech Co., Ltd. Firstly, 0.80 g of GO was uniformly dispersed in 160 mL of deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 30 minutes in an ice bath to prepare a suspension of graphite oxide water. Secondly, 2.40 g of Lys was dissolved in a 1×10−5 mol/L NaOH solution (pH=9). Then, the two dispersions were mixed and stirred for 20 minutes. Subsequently, the mixed solution underwent ultrasonic reaction at 80 °C for 3 h in a water bath. After that, it was washed and centrifuged three times with a mixture of ethanol and water (weight ratio of 1:1) to remove unreacted Lysine. Finally, the material was further processed by sonication to prepare an aqueous solution for storage. The synthesized GQD was analyzed by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) as shown in Fig. 1(b). Where, C is the element in the carbon group, N is the element in the amino group, O is the element in the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and Na is the element in the carboxyl group. TEM (FEI, Tecnai F30) and SEM (HITACHI, SU8010) images of GQD are shown in Figure S1 and S2 in the Supplementary Information.

TFBG was fabricated using the phase mask method. A high-energy pulse ultraviolet laser generated by an excimer laser (Coherent Inc., IC300 MOTOFRED), passing through a tilted phase mask, was directed onto a hydrogen-loaded photosensitive optical fiber. The laser exposure occurred point by point, creating a permanent periodic refractive index modulation. The ultraviolet laser had a wavelength of 278 nm, and the phase mask period is 542 nm. Finally, the TFBG with a grating length of 10 mm and a tilt angle of 8° was annealed in a temperature chamber at 120 °C for 12 h to release the residual hydrogen gas inside the optical fiber.

An optically driven deposition was employed as the coating method [43]. Initially, the TFBG was wiped with anhydrous ethanol to remove surface impurities. Subsequently, the TFBG was immersed in the aqueous solution of GQD, while simultaneously exposing it to a 1550 nm laser for 30 minutes. Finally, the coated TFBG was placed in a drying oven at 50 °C for 12 hours. Due to the optical pressure and heating effects of the laser, GQD could deposit on the surface of the TFBG grating region. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the deposition effect of GQD on optical fibers as a result of thin film deposition, as shown in Fig. 1(d).

The mode coupling in TFBG is a kind of backward coupling, wherein the forward-propagating core mode couples to the backward-propagating cladding modes. The coupling efficiency η can be expressed as [26,44]:
(1)
η =
tanh
2
κ L
where κ represents the coupling coefficient, and L denotes the length of the grating. Starting from the transverse component of the electric field of the model, κ is expressed as:
(2)
κ = C
∬
− ∞
+ ∞
E
→
core
*
E
→
r
∆ n
x , y
dx
dy
(3)
∆ n
x , y
= ∆ ncos
4 π
Λ
zcos θ + ysin θ
#
where C is a proportionality constant related to the normalization of the transverse field modes (
E
→
core
*
and
E
→
r
),
∆ n
x , y
is a function representing the external refractive index perturbation on the optical fiber cross-sectional grating, and θ is the tilt angle.

When GQD absorbs water molecules, the water molecules interact with the hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl, epoxy, amino and carboxyl groups) of GQD through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. These interactions may lead to structural changes in GQD, including alterations in spacing and conformation of functional groups. These changes can alter the distribution of charges within GQD, increase polarity, and influence the effective refractive index of both the real and imaginary parts of GQD film [45].

The structure of the proposed humidity sensor with GQD deposited on the TFBG is depicted in Fig. 1(c). When GQD adsorbs or desorbs water molecules, the refractive index of GQD undergoes changes, influencing the function
∆ n
x , y
of external refractive index perturbations on the optical fiber cross-sectional grating. This alteration affects the coupling efficiency η between the core mode and cladding modes.

The experimental setup for the humidity sensor based on GQD is illustrated in Fig. 1(e). The experimental setup was divided into two parts: the humidity control device and the optical fiber sensor system. In the humidity control device, there were two air pathways. One pathway passed through a water reservoir, generating wet air. The other pathway passed through a desiccant container, producing dry air. These two air pathways converged through a Y-tube into a single pathway, which enters the gas chamber (Material: PMMA, Size: 160 mm×80 mm×80 mm). By adjusting the power of pumps, the mixing ratio of wet and dry air was controlled. The RH in the chamber was regulated by monitoring the reading on the hygrometer ((Renke, COS-03) within the chamber. In the optical pathway, to capture the complete spectrum of the TFBG, a broadband light source (BBS: HOYATEK) with a wavelength range of 1400–1620 nm was utilized. The output light from the broadband source first passed through a polarization controller (PC: PR2000, JDS Uniphase), transforming it into P-polarized light, before reaching to the TFBG. The TFBG was positioned inside a chamber to detect changes in humidity within the chamber. The transmitted spectrum of TFBG was recorded by an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA: AQ6370D, YOKOGAWA, resolution: 0.02 nm). The wavelength resolution of the OSA was set as 0.05 nm. The laboratory temperature was automatically controlled by an air conditioning system and all experiments were conducted at room temperature 25℃.

The sensitivity of resonant modes varies due to varying intensities in the cladding modes and changes in the environmental refractive index. Furthermore, uneven deposition of functional materials may also influence the sensitivity of these modes to refractive index changes. Thus, in our experiments, we evaluated multiple modes and chose the one with the highest sensitivity for humidity measurement. The RH response results for different resonant modes were shown in Figure S3. Therefore, the selected resonance wavelength was about 1516.45 nm. The transmission spectra of the TFBG with RH controlled at 20 % and 90 % is displayed in Fig. 2(a). The detail spectrum change of the cladding mode at 1516.45 nm corresponding to the RH alteration was illustrated in the inset image. From the experimental data, it can be observed that the changes in light intensity caused by RH alteration at the wavelength of the troughs of cladding modes are roughly equivalent. Therefore, the change in trough intensity at a wavelength of 1516.46 nm was selected for data analysis and the intensity changes from −20.241 dB at 20 % RH to −18.546 dB at 90 % RH, resulting in a change of 1.695 dB.

Besides, the humidity response was researched with the RH increased from 20 % to 90 % with an increment of 10 %. During the experiment, each RH point was maintained for 5 minutes before measuring the spectrum to ensure the accuracy of the environmental humidity. The trough of the cladding mode at wavelength of 1516.45 was exhibited in Fig. 2(b). As external RH increased, little wavelength shift was observed, which means that the wavelength demodulation method is unsuitable for detecting RH changes. However, the intensity of the cladding mode was greatly influenced by external humidity. Fig. 2(c) illustrates the intensity changes of the core mode at different RH levels. The linear fitting of the intensity, with a slope of 0, indicates that the intensity of the core mode is not influenced by changes in RH. The core mode only propagates within the core and is not sensitive to changes in the refractive index outside the cladding. It is affected only by external temperature and stress. The response of the core mode to external temperature is almost consistent with that of the cladding mode. As a result, the cross-interference of temperature with humidity, which is a critical factor for humidity sensors, can be compensated by the calculation of temperature sensitivity of the core mode.

The light intensity at the wavelength of 1516.45 was shown in Fig. 2(d-e). The experiment data was measured three times and error bar was calculated as the humidity change between 20 % and 90 %. In the experiment, it can be observed that in the RH range of 20–40 %, the sensor response is smaller than that in the range of 40–90 % RH. This indicates that GQD exhibits low sensitivity to RH changes in dry environments with RH under 40 %. Besides, linear fitting was conducted based on the RH data ranging from 40–90 %. In the humidity decreasing and increasing progresses, the linear fit relationship between the cladding mode intensity at 1516.45 nm and RH were calculated, respectively. The sensitivities in the humidity decreasing and increasing progress were 0.027 dB/%RH and 0.032 dB/%RH respectively. The differences of the light intensity at each RH measuring point in the humidity increasing and decreasing progress were exhibited at Fig. 2(f). Furthermore, the humidity sensor displays minimal hysteresis effects and exhibits robust recovery capabilities during both the humidification and dehumidification stages.

Time response is a crucial parameter for humidity sensors as it influences the sensor ability to detect and respond to changes in humidity. The faster the time response, the better the real-time capability of the sensor in detecting of humidity variation. For fast-response humidity sensors, rapidly switching environmental humidity is key factor to measuring their equilibrium response speed. To address this issue, the chamber used in the sensor response speed detection was replaced with a slender pipeline (Material: silicone) with an outer diameter of 5 mm, an inner diameter of 4 mm, and a total length of 200 mm. The gas flow control method was exhibited in Figure S4. This modification will shrink the volume of the chamber and allows for rapid changes in the humidity environment surrounding the sensor through simple opening and closing of valves. The OSA was set to a single-point scanning mode to record changes in cladding mode intensity at 1516.45 nm. The scan included 1001 points with a scanning interval of 0.06 s, resulting in an overall test duration of 60 s. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 3(a). In this work, T90 rule was adopted for the calculation of response and recovery time. The output air from the water reservoir and the desiccant container correspond to the highest and lowest achievable humidity levels, respectively. These humidity levels were quantitatively assessed using a hygrometer, which recorded values of 95 % and 7 % respectively. When environmental RH undergoes rapid fluctuations between 7 % and 95 %, the interval required for the sensor to detect a change in light intensity achieving 90 % of its maximum response is defined as response time of the proposed sensor in this work. The humidity change time interval was set as 15 s. Within three humidity switching cycles, the sensor not only demonstrated high stability but also showcased its rapid response and recovery capabilities. The average response and recovery time of the proposed sensor was calculated as 3.66 s and 0.92 s, respectively. The response and recovery time of fiber optic humidity sensors in recent years is summarized and illustrated in Fig. 3(b). Detailed data of the response time performances of fiber humidity sensors in recent years are available in the Table S1 of the Supplementary information. The GQD enabled fiber optic humidity sensor proposed in this work has demonstrated rapid recovery time reaching to a new area that less than 1 s. The experimentally detected recovery time exceeding the response time suggests that the rate of desorption of water molecules by the sensor is greater than the adsorption rate. This phenomenon may result from the combined influence of the intrinsic properties of GQD and the specific characteristics of the film formation on the optical fiber surface.

Time stability is also crucial for fiber optic humidity sensors because it directly affects the accuracy of humidity measurements. Sensors with high stability can provide more consistent and reliable humidity measurement results. In non-contact sensing application, the sensor must maintain sufficient stability and consistency over a certain period to ensure accurate tracking of finger position.

To assess the stability of the sensor, stability tests were conducted for half an hour in different humidity environments. The sensor was placed in a chamber with RH was controlled at 40 % and 80 %. Then the spectra were recorded every 3 minutes. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 3(c). In the 40 % RH environment, the maximum error in intensity was ±0.16×10−3 dB, corresponding to a humidity measurement accuracy fluctuation of ±0.5 % RH. In the 80 % RH environment, the maximum error in intensity was ±0.48×10−3 dB, corresponding to a humidity measurement accuracy fluctuation of ±1.5 % RH. In order to verify the long-term stability of the sensor, a one-week stability test was conducted. The intensities response results of the resonant wavelength of 1516.45 nm are shown in Fig. 3(d), where red triangles, yellow quadrilaterals, green pentagons, and blue hexagons respectively represent the sensor responses measured on the first, third, fifth, and seventh days. We calculated error bars and plotted the results in the inset of Fig. 3(d). During the seven-day stability test, the sensor demonstrated low error. The experimental results indicate that our sensor possesses good temporal stability. At different testing times, the whole spectral responses of the TFBG to humidity increases from 20 % to 90 %, in increments of 10 %, are displayed in Figure S5.

The skin surface is constantly evaporating sweat, allowing a high relative humidity at the sweat gland outlets. Therefore, the humidity gradient between the surface of the finger and the ambient relative humidity can be assessed by measuring the RH level, which allows for the determination of the spatial information of the finger. Furthermore, decoding the position information can obtain the action of finger. The working principle is illustrated in Fig. 4(a). By detecting changes in humidity caused by the proximity of the fingertip, the sensor can decode the movement information of the fingertip without direct contact.

In the non-contact sensing experiment, the maximum response distance of the sensor is measured to be 10 mm. Six sets of experiments were designed in this study to evaluate the ability of the sensor to perceive finger position and movement. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 4(b). During the non-contact sensing process of the humidity sensor with the finger, the movement of the finger can be categorized into three states: approaching, hovering, and moving away. In all tests, the experimental results effectively reflected the finger movement states. When the finger approached the sensor, the intensity increased rapidly. When the finger stayed in the approaching or leaving process, the change in intensity was relatively slow. When the finger left the sensor, the intensity decreased rapidly.

Test-1, Test-2 and Test-3 correspond to different distances (L are about 1 mm, 3 mm, 7 mm, respectively) of finger proximity to the sensor. There is a significant difference in the intensity response of the cladding mode at 1516.45 nm for the above three distances. The results show that the sensor can respond well to the distance from the finger to the sensor, and the intensity response is negatively correlated with the distance from the finger to the sensor. Test-4 is an experiment in which the finger is repeatedly approaching and leaving the sensor, and the results indicate that the sensor responds well to the back-and-forth reciprocal process. Test-5 and Test-6 are based on Test-4 with the addition of the stay state. The difference is that the finger was closer to the sensor in the Test-5 experiment (L is about 1–7 mm), while in Test-6 it was at a longer distance (L is about 8–10 mm). It can be seen that the proposed sensor can response well to all these movement states with clear distinctions of the intensities of the resonant wavelength in the spectra.

In summary, a humidity sensor based on TFBG coated with GQD film for a non-contact finger action perception was proposed. The proposed sensor demonstrated good stability and a fast response in experiments, aligning well with the requirements of non-contact sensing. After absorbing water molecules, GQD exhibited a change in refractive index, affecting the intensity of TFBG cladding modes. The proposed sensor showed a high sensitivity of 0.032 dB/% RH with a rapid response and recovery time of 3.66 s and 0.92 s. In non-contact sensing experiments, six different finger movement states could be detected accurately by the sensor, especially for the distinguishing between the movement states such as approaching, hovering, and moving away. Moreover, the sensor can respond within a range of approximately 10 mm. The proposed sensor is expected to be used for non-contact control of human-machine interaction in artificial intelligence.

This work was supported in part by National Natural Science Foundation of China (12274386), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LQ23F050006), Key R & D project of Zhejiang Province (2021C01179), National Key R & D Program of China (2021YFF0600203) and Fundamental Research Funds of China Jiliang University (2023YW74).

Changyu Shen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenjun Zhou: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Jie Dong: Software, Investigation. Jun Zhou: Validation, Formal analysis. Ziqiao Ren: Visualization, Data curation. Jun Wu: Validation, Formal analysis. Jianing Kang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Feng Gao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fuxiang Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yufei Shi: Visualization, Software, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible noncontact sensing for human-machine interaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.J. Lu, C.P. Jiang, G.S. Hu, J.Q. Liu, B. Yang, Flexible Noncontact Sensing for Human-Machine Interaction, Adv. Mater. 33(2021) e2100218.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization and optimization of level measurement by an ultrasonic sensor system

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance humidity sensor based on graphitic carbon nitride/polyethylene oxide and construction of sensor array for non-contact humidity detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of fabrication technique and sensing performance of optical fiber humidity sensors in the most recent decade

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yufei Shi is a research scholar at China Jiliang University, Hangzhou City, China, where he is currently pursuing a Master degree in Electronic Information Engineering. He received his B.S. degree in Optics from Qilu University of technology in 2022. His research interests include humidity sensors, in-fiber interferometer, tilted fiber Bragg grating.

Fuxiang Wang is a research scholar at China Jiliang University, Hangzhou City, China, where he is currently pursuing a Master degree in Electronic Information Engineering. He received his B.S. degree in Optics from China Jiliang University in 2021. His research interests include humidity sensors, tilted fiber Bragg grating.

Feng Gao received the Ph.D. degree from School of Optical and Electronic Information, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. Since 2018, he is currently a Lecturer in College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou, China. His research interests include optical fiber sensors and nanomaterials.

Jun Zhou received the M.S. degree from Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanic, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2013 and the Ph.D. degree in control science and engineering from Zhejiang University of Technology in 2022. He is currently a Lecturer in College of Optical and Electronic Technology, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou, China. His research interests include fiber optic sensors and laser display and imaging.

Changyu Shen received the B.S. and M.S. degrees in Optics from Shaanxi Normal University (China) in 2002 and the Ph.D. degree in Optical Engineering from Zhejiang University (China) in 2009. In 2013 and 2017, he worked at Carleton University (Canada) and Trinity College Dublin (Ireland), as a visiting scholar, respectively. From 2015–2017, he worked at the College of Optical Science and Engineering of Zhejiang University (China), as postdoctoral fellow. He is currently a Professor with China Jiliang University (China). His research interests include tilted fiber Bragg grating sensors, surface plasma resonance fiber optical sensors, hydrogen fiber optical sensors, and electromagnetic testing technique.
